title: Phyla strigulosa
taxonomy: Phyla strigulosa belongs to the family Verbenaceae and the order Lamiales. This classification places it within the larger group of flowering plants known as angiosperms. The genus Phyla is typically considered to be a part of the subfamily Verbenoideae. The species P. strigulosa is a member of the Verbenaceae family, which contains about 150 genera. The Verbenaceae family is characterized by its diverse range of shrubs, trees, and herbs.
uses: Phyla strigulosa, also known as the pineland bluecurl, is a plant species native to the southeastern United States.  It has been used in traditional folk medicine for various purposes, although its effectiveness has not been extensively scientifically studied. Traditionally, the plant has been used to treat a range of ailments, including fever, sore throats, and skin conditions. The plant's leaves and stems are often used in herbal remedies, and it is also used in the treatment of respiratory issues. Further research is needed to fully understand the medicinal properties and potential uses of Phyla strigulosa.

intro: Phyla strigulosa, also known as lophostemone or frog apple, is a perennial herb belonging to the Lamiaceae family, native to the southeastern United States. This plant has been used in traditional medicine for its purported health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may help to alleviate respiratory issues and skin infections. The bioactive constituents of Phyla strigulosa include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made from this plant include teas, infusions, and tinctures, often used to treat coughs, colds, and skin conditions.
